***Hop*** is a 2011 American [live-action animated](/wiki/Live-action_animated_film "Live-action animated film") [Easter](/wiki/List_of_Easter_films "List of Easter films") [comedy film](/wiki/Comedy_film "Comedy film") directed by [Tim Hill](/wiki/Tim_Hill_(filmmaker) "Tim Hill (filmmaker)") and written by [Brian Lynch](/wiki/Brian_Lynch_(writer) "Brian Lynch (writer)"), [Cinco Paul, and Ken Daurio](/wiki/Cinco_Paul_and_Ken_Daurio "Cinco Paul and Ken Daurio"). Produced by [Illumination Entertainment](/wiki/Illumination_Entertainment "Illumination Entertainment"), it stars [James Marsden](/wiki/James_Marsden "James Marsden"), [Kaley Cuoco](/wiki/Kaley_Cuoco "Kaley Cuoco"), [Gary Cole](/wiki/Gary_Cole "Gary Cole"), [Elizabeth Perkins](/wiki/Elizabeth_Perkins "Elizabeth Perkins"), [David Hasselhoff](/wiki/David_Hasselhoff "David Hasselhoff"), and [Chelsea Handler](/wiki/Chelsea_Handler "Chelsea Handler"), with the voices of [Russell Brand](/wiki/Russell_Brand "Russell Brand"), [Hank Azaria](/wiki/Hank_Azaria "Hank Azaria"), and [Hugh Laurie](/wiki/Hugh_Laurie "Hugh Laurie"). The film follows a young rabbit who would rather drum in a band than succeed his father as the Easter Bunny, and befriends a human slacker seeking a job.

*Hop* premiered at [Universal Studios Hollywood](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Hollywood "Universal Studios Hollywood") on March 27, 2011, and was theatrically released in the United States on April 1 by [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"). It received mixed reviews from critics but was a box-office success, grossing $188.5 million against a budget of $63 million.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

On [Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island"), a young rabbit named E.B. is intended to succeed his father as the [Easter Bunny](/wiki/Easter_Bunny "Easter Bunny"). Ignoring his father's orders, E.B. runs away to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") to pursue his dream of becoming a music drummer. In [Van Nuys](/wiki/Van_Nuys,_California "Van Nuys, California"), E.B. is hit by Fred O'Hare, an out-of-work, job-hopping slacker who was driving to his sister Sam's boss's house to house-sit after his parents forced him to move out. Feigning injury, E.B. persuades Fred to take him in as he recovers, but when E.B. causes trouble, Fred attempts to release him in the wilderness. However, E.B. persuades Fred to let him stay, claiming to be the Easter Bunny, whom Fred had witnessed delivering eggs as a child, becoming infatuated with Easter ever since.

Meanwhile, E.B.'s father sends his royal guards, the Pink Berets, to search for him and bring him back. In Hollywood, E.B. sees the Berets closing in on him and hides inside a business where Fred is having a job interview. E.B. enjoys a successful recording session with [The Blind Boys of Alabama](/wiki/The_Blind_Boys_of_Alabama "The Blind Boys of Alabama") as their substitute drummer, but ruins Fred's job interview. In the process, E.B. gets a tip about a possible audition for [David Hasselhoff](/wiki/David_Hasselhoff "David Hasselhoff"), and after performing for him, he invites him to perform on his show.

Afterwards, Fred attends his adoptive younger sister Alex's school Easter pageant with E.B. hiding in a satchel. E.B., believing that the Pink Berets have found him due to the three bunny suit shadows on a wall and disgusted by Alex's awful rendition of "[Peter Cottontail](/wiki/Peter_Cottontail_(song) "Peter Cottontail (song)")", dashes out and disrupts the show. Fred fakes being a ventriloquist with E.B. as his dummy, and the two upstage the show singing, "[I Want Candy](/wiki/I_Want_Candy "I Want Candy")". Both Fred's father Henry and Alex are angry and upset about the upstaging, but Fred is inspired to be the Easter Bunny himself. Although skeptical, E.B. agrees to train him and finds that Fred has some genuine talent for it.

Meanwhile, the Easter Bunny's best friend and [second-in-command](/wiki/Second-in-command "Second-in-command") Carlos the Chick secretly plots a [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") against him to take over Easter, inspiring the chicks to uprise against the bunnies, and begins training to become the "Easter Chick". The next morning, as E.B. is about to go to Hasselhoff's show, he notices the Pink Berets and prepares a decoy to fake his death, leaving Fred behind. The Berets see the decoy and, horrified that Fred has apparently killed E.B., capture him and take him to Easter Island. Fred is confronted by E.B.'s father and Carlos, who seizes control of the Easter factory, tying up E.B.'s father and placing him and Fred to be boiled alive. Meanwhile, E.B. starts to feel guilty for leaving Fred, and is convinced by Hasselhoff on his show to go back and help his friend.

E.B. races back to the factory to confront Carlos, but he is immobilized in gummy candy and tossed into the chocolate bunny carving line. E.B. survives by dodging the blades of the machine, while Fred eats through the black-licorice ropes, escaping with E.B's father. Carlos turns into a mutant chick-bunny hybrid due to the magic of the Egg of Destiny, and battles with E.B., defeating him easily due to his size. Carlos then tries to lead the Egg Sleigh out with one of his assistants Phil directing, but E.B. improvises a drum session that drives Phil to uncontrollably dance to the beat and provide the wrong signals, causing the sleigh to crash and subdue Carlos. E.B. and his father reconcile, and he and Fred are crowned co-Easter bunnies. Back at home, Fred reconciles with his father and his family, and then takes off with E.B.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Production")]

### Animation and character design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Animation and character design")]

The animated characters were designed by [Peter de Sève](/wiki/Peter_de_S%C3%A8ve "Peter de Sève").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The [CGI](/wiki/Computer_generated_imagery "Computer generated imagery") animation and visual effects for the film were produced by Los Angeles-based [Rhythm & Hues Studios](/wiki/Rhythm_%26_Hues_Studios "Rhythm & Hues Studios") (R&H).[[18]](#cite_note-18) Ray Chen was the visual effects supervisor, and Andy Arnett was the animation director.[[19]](#cite_note-fxguide-19) Rhythm & Hues looked at the actions of real rabbits for some scenes. Arnett said, "One of the things that can happen with these faces is that if you don't put a lot of detail into the entire face, their mouths and cheeks can look kind of stiff. So it was important to us to be able to get a lot of flexibility in the face to make them feel alive and fleshy."[[19]](#cite_note-fxguide-19)

The easter egg factory involved Rhythm & Hues completing 250 computer-generated shots. Chen notes, "It's almost like a little full CG film inside the big film. The biggest challenge there was actually designing the factory. It originally had more of an underground feel with roots and rocks before we settled on something that looked more cathedral-like."[[19]](#cite_note-fxguide-19)

***Hop (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack)*** is the complete soundtrack album for the film. The entire score was composed by award-winning composer [Christopher Lennertz](/wiki/Christopher_Lennertz "Christopher Lennertz"), who had previously composed the score for *[Alvin and the Chipmunks](/wiki/Alvin_and_the_Chipmunks_(film) "Alvin and the Chipmunks (film)")*, another film directed by [Tim Hill](/wiki/Tim_Hill_(filmmaker) "Tim Hill (filmmaker)"). The album was released on March 29, 2011, by [Back Lot Music](/wiki/Back_Lot_Music "Back Lot Music") and [Varèse Sarabande Records](/wiki/Var%C3%A8se_Sarabande "Varèse Sarabande").

A 35th track, titled "The Pink Berets", is included in the soundtrack. Christopher Lennertz wrote the song with Ali Theodore, Bryan Spitzer, Jordan Yaeger, and Julian Michael Davis. The Deekompressors performed the song. Australian pop singer [Cody Simpson](/wiki/Cody_Simpson "Cody Simpson") performed a cover of [the Strangeloves](/wiki/The_Strangeloves "The Strangeloves")' 1965 standard "[I Want Candy](/wiki/I_Want_Candy "I Want Candy")" for the film (with [Bow Wow Wow](/wiki/Bow_Wow_Wow "Bow Wow Wow")'s 1982 version previously being featured in some trailers for the film).

The Japanese dub features an exclusive theme song called "Hug Tomo" by [Not Yet](/wiki/Not_Yet_(band) "Not Yet (band)").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Release")]

### Promotion and marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Promotion and marketing")]

Universal teamed up with 92 major companies to promote *Hop*, including [Holiday Inn](/wiki/Holiday_Inn "Holiday Inn"), [Krispy Kreme](/wiki/Krispy_Kreme "Krispy Kreme"), [Lindt](/wiki/Lindt "Lindt"), [Kraft Foods](/wiki/Kraft_Foods "Kraft Foods"), [The Hershey Company](/wiki/The_Hershey_Company "The Hershey Company"), [Build-A-Bear Workshop](/wiki/Build-A-Bear_Workshop "Build-A-Bear Workshop"), [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast") (which acquired Universal's parent company [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") two months before the film was released), [Kodak](/wiki/Kodak "Kodak"), [Hallmark](/wiki/Hallmark "Hallmark"), [HMV](/wiki/HMV "HMV"), and [Burger King](/wiki/Burger_King "Burger King"). A large range of licensed merchandise was released in connection with the film, including toys, stuffed animals, many sorts of candy, T-shirts, cookie decorating kits, baked goods and other products from [Kraft Foods](/wiki/Kraft_Foods "Kraft Foods"). Some items were available exclusively at [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart") stores.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The premiere of *Hop* took place at [Universal Studios Hollywood](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Hollywood "Universal Studios Hollywood") on March 27, 2011.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

A [tie-in](/wiki/Tie-in "Tie-in") video game was released for the [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS") alongside the film.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) On March 29, 2011, a crossover game between *Hop* and *[Doodle Jump](/wiki/Doodle_Jump "Doodle Jump")* was released on the [App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(Apple) "App Store (Apple)") for [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") devices.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) As part of *Doodle Jump*'s [in-app purchases](/wiki/In-app_purchases "In-app purchases"), a new level can be selected through naming at *Hop*'s [game over screen](/wiki/Game_over_screen "Game over screen").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Home media")]

[Universal Pictures Home Entertainment](/wiki/Universal_Pictures_Home_Entertainment "Universal Pictures Home Entertainment") released *Hop* on [DVD](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") on March 23, 2012.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Physical copies include featurettes and games,[[30]](#cite_note-30) with a short film *Phil's Dance Party*.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Theme parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Theme parks")]

Starting in April 2011, characters from the film would appear at [Universal Studios Florida](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Florida "Universal Studios Florida") and Universal Studios Hollywood for shows and meet and greets to help promote the film.

The film would get its own unit in [Universal's Superstar Parade](/wiki/Universal%27s_Superstar_Parade "Universal's Superstar Parade") at Universal Studios Florida in May 2012, before it would be replaced by a [*Secret Life of Pets*](/wiki/The_Secret_Life_of_Pets "The Secret Life of Pets")-themed unit in November 2016.

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Hop* earned $108.1 million in the United States and Canada and $80.4 million in other countries, for a worldwide total of $188.5 million.[[6]](#cite_note-NUM-6) It was the 39th-highest-grossing film of 2011.[[32]](#cite_note-32) To date, *Hop* is the lowest-grossing film released by Illumination.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In the United States and Canada, *Hop* was released on April 1, 2011.[[5]](#cite_note-BOM-5) It earned $11.5 million on its first day. The film debuted earning $38 million across 3,579 theaters.[[34]](#cite_note-OpeningWeekend-34) Its second weekend earnings dropped by 42 percent[[35]](#cite_note-DeadlineSecondWeekend-35) to $21.7 million,[[36]](#cite_note-36) and followed by another $11.1 million the third weekend.[[37]](#cite_note-37) *Hop* left theaters by August 19, 2011, making it the year's 25th-highest-grossing film.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Critical response")]

*Hop* received mixed reviews from critics, with praise for the animation, music, and performances from the cast, particularly Marsden,[[39]](#cite_note-TheLedger-39)[[40]](#cite_note-CriticalCritics-40) but criticism for its humor, pacing and script.[[39]](#cite_note-TheLedger-39)[[40]](#cite_note-CriticalCritics-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-IGN-42) The [review aggregation](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") reported that out of 137 critic reviews, 25% of them were positive, with an average rating of 4.3/10. The site's consensus states: "It's impressively animated, but *Hop'*s script is so uninspired that not even James Marsden's frantic mugging can give it any bounce."[[43]](#cite_note-43) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic") assigned the film a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_average "Weighted average") score of 41 out of 100, based on 23 reviews, indicating "mixed or average" reviews.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A−" on an A+ to F scale.[[35]](#cite_note-DeadlineSecondWeekend-35)

The subplot involving Carlos the Easter Chick was considered to be insensitive to [Mexican Americans](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") by one reviewer.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) Peter Debruge of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* called it "hilariously un-[PC](/wiki/Political_correctness "Political correctness")".[[9]](#cite_note-VarietyReview-9) [Philip French](/wiki/Philip_French "Philip French") of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* wrote: "It's a combination of the worst movies about trouble in Santa Claus's north pole toy factory and a version of *[Charlie and the Chocolate Factory](/wiki/Charlie_and_the_Chocolate_Factory_(film) "Charlie and the Chocolate Factory (film)")* lethally laced with sugar."[[47]](#cite_note-Guardian-47) Also writing for *The Guardian*, [Peter Bradshaw](/wiki/Peter_Bradshaw "Peter Bradshaw") gave it one star out of four and called it a "soulless and depressing film, with plasticky production design."[[48]](#cite_note-Guardian2-48)

## Accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hop_(film)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Accolades")]

*Hop* was nominated in two categories at the 2011 [Golden Trailer Awards](/wiki/Golden_Trailer_Awards "Golden Trailer Awards"): "Battle For Easter" (Cimarron Entertainment) for Best Animation/Family and "Carrot Quiz" (Mojo) for Best Animation/Family TV Spot.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Character animator Andrew Arnett was nominated for [Outstanding Achievement for Character Animation in a Live Action Production](/wiki/Annie_Award_for_Outstanding_Achievement_for_Character_Animation_in_a_Live_Action_Production "Annie Award for Outstanding Achievement for Character Animation in a Live Action Production") at the [39th Annie Awards](/wiki/39th_Annie_Awards "39th Annie Awards").[[50]](#cite_note-50) At the [Golden Reel Awards](/wiki/Golden_Reel_Award_(Motion_Picture_Sound_Editors) "Golden Reel Award (Motion Picture Sound Editors)") 2011, the film received a nomination for [Outstanding Achievement in Sound Editing – Feature Underscore](/wiki/Golden_Reel_Award_for_Outstanding_Achievement_in_Sound_Editing_%E2%80%93_Feature_Underscore "Golden Reel Award for Outstanding Achievement in Sound Editing – Feature Underscore").[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)
